sources.notes: National Archives milestone document page for the G.I. Bill. Also notes that Black veterans often faced discrimination in accessing mortgages and housing opportunities.

sources.source_title: The 80th Anniversary of the G.I. Bill
sources.notes: Archival overview of the G.I. Bill's purpose and its role in helping veterans transition to civilian life.

sources.source_title: The G.I. Bill, World War II, and the Education of Black Americans
sources.notes: Research summary explaining that while Black veterans were eligible for educational benefits, segregation and limited capacity at Black colleges constrained access, especially in the South.

metrics.metric_name: Veterans who used G.I. Bill education or training benefits by 1956
metrics.methodology_note: National Archives historical summary states that by 1956 the G.I. Bill had helped 7.8 million veterans pursue education or training. This is a broad reach metric, not a race-specific equity metric.

sources.notes: Federal Reserve history of the CRA describing its response to redlining, disinvestment, and inequities in access to credit.
Context page linking the CRA to the Federal Reserve's role in community lending, access to credit, and low- and moderate-income communities.

sources.source_title: The Federal Reserve's Community Development Function
sources.notes: Explains how the CRA shaped the Federal Reserve's later community development responsibilities and compliance role.

sources.source_title: Crime Prevention and the African-American Community
sources.notes: USCCR report discussing the crack versus powder cocaine sentencing disparity created by the 1986 law and its disproportionate impact on Black communities.

sources.source_title: The Crisis of the Young African American Male in the Inner Cities
sources.notes: USCCR report describing how drug policies and sentencing practices deepened racial disparities in incarceration and community outcomes.

sources.source_title: Attorney General Memorandum for All Federal Prosecutors Concerning the Application of the Statutory Mandatory Minimum Sentencing Laws for Crack Cocaine Offenses Amended by the Fair Sentencing Act of 2010
sources.notes: DOJ memorandum summarizing the old crack cocaine mandatory minimum thresholds that originated under the 1986 law and were later reduced by the Fair Sentencing Act.

metrics.metric_name: Crack-to-powder cocaine mandatory minimum sentencing ratio
metrics.methodology_note: Represents the 100-to-1 disparity embedded in federal sentencing policy for crack versus powder cocaine offenses under the Anti-Drug Abuse Act of 1986.

sources.source_title: Attorney General Memorandum for All Federal Prosecutors Concerning the Application of the Statutory Mandatory Minimum Sentencing Laws for Crack Cocaine Offenses Amended by the Fair Sentencing Act of 2010
sources.notes: DOJ explanation of how the Fair Sentencing Act changed the crack cocaine threshold quantities tied to federal mandatory minimums.
DOJ memorandum stating the revised crack cocaine thresholds for five- and ten-year mandatory minimum penalties.

sources.source_title: Justice Department Set to Expand Clemency Criteria, Will Prepare Wave of Additional Clefts
sources.notes: DOJ statement describing the Fair Sentencing Act as reducing the disparity between crack and powder cocaine offenses.

metrics.metric_name: Crack-to-powder cocaine mandatory minimum sentencing ratio after the Fair Sentencing Act
metrics.methodology_note: Measures the reduction in the statutory crack-to-powder sentencing disparity from 100-to-1 to 18-to-1 under the Fair Sentencing Act of 2010.
